From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Sand- to boulder-sized particle of debris in the Solar System

Not to be confused with [Metroid](/wiki/Metroid "Metroid"), [Meteor](/wiki/Meteor "Meteor"), or [Matroid](/wiki/Matroid "Matroid").

"Meteoro" redirects here. For other uses, see [Meteoro (disambiguation)](/wiki/Meteoro_(disambiguation) "Meteoro (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Meteoroid_meteor_meteorite.gif/500px-Meteoroid_meteor_meteorite.gif)](/wiki/File:Meteoroid_meteor_meteorite.gif)

A *meteoroid* shown entering the atmosphere, causing a visible *[meteor](/wiki/Meteor "Meteor")* and hitting the Earth's surface, becoming a *[meteorite](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite")*

A **meteoroid** ([/ˈmiːtiərɔɪd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*MEE-tee-ə-royd*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a small body in [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space").
Meteoroids are distinguished as objects significantly smaller than *[asteroids](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid")*, ranging in size from grains to objects up to one meter (3.28 feet) wide.[[2]](#cite_note-Rubin2010-2) Objects smaller than meteoroids are classified as *[micrometeoroids](/wiki/Micrometeoroids "Micrometeoroids")* or *[space dust](/wiki/Space_dust "Space dust")*.[[2]](#cite_note-Rubin2010-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Many are fragments from [comets](/wiki/Comet "Comet") or asteroids, whereas others are [collision impact](/wiki/Impact_event "Impact event") [debris](/wiki/Space_debris "Space debris") ejected from bodies such as the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") or [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The visible passage of a meteoroid, comet, or asteroid [entering Earth's atmosphere](/wiki/Atmospheric_entry "Atmospheric entry") is called a [meteor](/wiki/Meteor "Meteor"), and a series of many meteors appearing seconds or minutes apart and appearing to originate from the same fixed point in the sky is called a [meteor shower](/wiki/Meteor_shower "Meteor shower").

An estimated 25 million meteoroids, micrometeoroids and other [space debris](/wiki/Space_debris "Space debris") enter Earth's atmosphere each day,[[8]](#cite_note-8) which results in an estimated 15,000 tonnes (16,535 short tons) of that material entering the atmosphere each year.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
A *[meteorite](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite")* is the remains of a meteoroid that has survived the [ablation](/wiki/Ablation "Ablation") of its surface material during its passage through the atmosphere as a meteor and has impacted the ground.

## Meteoroids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meteoroid&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Meteoroids")]

See also: [Micrometeoroid](/wiki/Micrometeoroid "Micrometeoroid")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Meteoroid_track_through_aerogel_from_EURECA_mission.jpg/250px-Meteoroid_track_through_aerogel_from_EURECA_mission.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meteoroid_track_through_aerogel_from_EURECA_mission.jpg)

Meteoroid embedded in [aerogel](/wiki/Aerogel "Aerogel"); the meteoroid is 10 [μm](/wiki/%CE%9Cm "Μm") in diameter and its track is 1.5 mm long

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/323213main_Petersmeteorites_946-710.jpg/250px-323213main_Petersmeteorites_946-710.jpg)](/wiki/File:323213main_Petersmeteorites_946-710.jpg)

[2008 TC3](/wiki/2008_TC3 "2008 TC3") [meteorite](#Meteoroids) fragments found on February 28, 2009, in the [Nubian Desert](/wiki/Nubian_Desert "Nubian Desert"), [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan")

In 1961, the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union") (IAU) defined a meteoroid as "a solid object moving in interplanetary space, of a size considerably smaller than an [asteroid](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") and considerably larger than an atom".[[10]](#cite_note-IMO-meteoroid-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Millman1961-11) In 1995, Beech and Steel, writing in the *[Quarterly Journal of the Royal Astronomical Society](/wiki/Quarterly_Journal_of_the_Royal_Astronomical_Society "Quarterly Journal of the Royal Astronomical Society")*, proposed a new definition where a meteoroid would be between 100 μm and 10 meters across.[[12]](#cite_note-Steel1995-12) In 2010, following the discovery of asteroids below 10 m in size, Rubin and Grossman proposed a revision of the previous definition of meteoroid to objects between 10 μm and one meter in diameter in order to maintain the distinction.[[2]](#cite_note-Rubin2010-2) According to Rubin and Grossman, the minimum size of an asteroid is given by what can be discovered from Earth-bound telescopes, so the distinction between meteoroid and asteroid is fuzzy. Some of the smallest asteroids discovered (based on [absolute magnitude](/wiki/Absolute_magnitude#Solar_System_bodies_(H) "Absolute magnitude") *H*) are [2008 TS26](/wiki/2008_TS26 "2008 TS26") with *H* = 33.2[[13]](#cite_note-smallest-jpl-13) and [2011 CQ1](/wiki/2011_CQ1 "2011 CQ1") with *H* = 32.1[[14]](#cite_note-14) both with an estimated size of one meter.[[15]](#cite_note-NASA-166-15) In April 2017, the IAU adopted an official revision of its definition, limiting size to between 30 μm (0.0012 in) and one meter in diameter, but allowing for a deviation for any object causing a meteor.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Objects smaller than meteoroids are classified as [micrometeoroids](/wiki/Micrometeoroid "Micrometeoroid") and [interplanetary dust](/wiki/Interplanetary_dust_cloud "Interplanetary dust cloud"). The [Minor Planet Center](/wiki/Minor_Planet_Center "Minor Planet Center") does not use the term "meteoroid".

### Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meteoroid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Composition")]

Almost all meteoroids contain extraterrestrial nickel and iron. They have three main classifications: iron, stone, and stony-iron. Some stone meteoroids contain grain-like inclusions known as [chondrules](/wiki/Chondrule "Chondrule") and are called [chondrites](/wiki/Chondrite "Chondrite"). Stony meteoroids without these features are called "[achondrites](/wiki/Achondrite "Achondrite")", which are typically formed from extraterrestrial igneous activity; they contain little or no extraterrestrial iron.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The composition of meteoroids can be inferred as they pass through Earth's atmosphere from their trajectories and the light spectra of the resulting meteor. Their effects on radio signals also give information, especially useful for daytime meteors, which are otherwise very difficult to observe. From these trajectory measurements, meteoroids have been found to have many different orbits, some clustering in streams *(see [meteor showers](/wiki/Meteor_showers "Meteor showers"))* often associated with a parent [comet](/wiki/Comet "Comet"), others apparently sporadic. Debris from meteoroid streams may eventually be scattered into other orbits. The light spectra, combined with trajectory and light curve measurements, have yielded various compositions and densities, ranging from fragile snowball-like objects with density about a quarter that of ice,[[18]](#cite_note-18) to nickel-iron rich dense rocks. The study of [meteorites](/wiki/Meteorite#Meteorite_types "Meteorite") also gives insights into the composition of non-ephemeral meteoroids.

### In the Solar System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meteoroid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In the Solar System")]

Most meteoroids come from the [asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt"), having been perturbed by the gravitational influences of planets, but others are particles from [comets](/wiki/Comet "Comet"), giving rise to [meteor showers](/wiki/Meteor_shower "Meteor shower"). Some meteoroids are fragments from bodies such as Mars or the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), that have been thrown into space by an impact.

Meteoroids travel around the Sun in a variety of orbits and at various velocities. The fastest move at about 42 km/s through space in the vicinity of Earth's orbit. This is [escape velocity](/wiki/Escape_velocity "Escape velocity") from the Sun, equal to the square root of two times Earth's speed, and is the upper speed limit of objects in the vicinity of Earth, unless they come from interstellar space. Earth travels at about 29.6 km/s, so when meteoroids meet the atmosphere head-on (which only occurs when meteors are in a [retrograde orbit](/wiki/Retrograde_orbit "Retrograde orbit") such as the [Leonids](/wiki/Leonids "Leonids"), which are associated with the retrograde comet [55P/Tempel–Tuttle](/wiki/55P/Tempel%E2%80%93Tuttle "55P/Tempel–Tuttle")) the combined speed may reach about 71 km/s (see [Specific energy#Astrodynamics](/wiki/Specific_energy#Astrodynamics "Specific energy")). Meteoroids moving through Earth's orbital space average about 20 km/s,[[19]](#cite_note-19) but due to Earth's gravity meteors such as the [Phoenicids](/wiki/Phoenicids "Phoenicids") can make atmospheric entry at as slow as about 11 km/s.

On January 17, 2013, at 05:21 PST, a one meter comet from the [Oort cloud](/wiki/Oort_cloud "Oort cloud") entered Earth atmosphere over [California](/wiki/California "California") and [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada").[[20]](#cite_note-CAMS-20) The object had a retrograde orbit with perihelion at 0.98 ± 0.03 [AU](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit"). It approached from the direction of the constellation [Virgo](/wiki/Virgo_(constellation) "Virgo (constellation)") (which was in the south about 50° above the horizon at the time), and collided head-on with Earth's atmosphere at 72 ± 6 km/s[[20]](#cite_note-CAMS-20) vaporising more than 100 km above ground over a period of several seconds.

### Collision with Earth's atmosphere

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meteoroid&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Collision with Earth's atmosphere")]

Main article: [Meteor](/wiki/Meteor "Meteor")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Meteoroid_meteor_meteorite.gif/250px-Meteoroid_meteor_meteorite.gif)](/wiki/File:Meteoroid_meteor_meteorite.gif)

Animated illustration of different phases as a meteoroid enters the Earth's atmosphere to become visible as a [meteor](/wiki/Meteor "Meteor") and land as a [meteorite](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite")

When meteoroids intersect with Earth's atmosphere at night, they are likely to become visible as [meteors](#Meteors). If meteoroids survive the entry through the atmosphere and reach Earth's surface, they are called [meteorites](#Meteorites). Meteorites are transformed in structure and chemistry by the heat of entry and force of impact. A noted 4-meter (13 ft) [asteroid](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid"), [2008 TC3](/wiki/2008_TC3 "2008 TC3"), was observed in space on a collision course with Earth on 6 October 2008 and entered Earth's atmosphere the next day, striking a remote area of northern Sudan. It was the first time that a meteoroid had been observed in space and tracked prior to impacting Earth.[[10]](#cite_note-IMO-meteoroid-10) [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") has produced a map showing the most notable asteroid collisions with Earth and its atmosphere from 1994 to 2013 from data gathered by U.S. government sensors.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Meteorites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meteoroid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Meteorites")]

Main article: [Meteorite](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Murnpeowie_meteorite.jpg/250px-Murnpeowie_meteorite.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murnpeowie_meteorite.jpg)

Murnpeowie [meteorite](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite"), an [iron meteorite](/wiki/Iron_meteorite "Iron meteorite") with [regmaglypts](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/regmaglypt "wikt:regmaglypt") resembling thumbprints (Australia, 1910)

A meteorite is a portion of a meteoroid or asteroid that survives its passage through the atmosphere and hits the ground without being destroyed.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Meteorites are sometimes, but not always, found in association with hypervelocity [impact craters](/wiki/Impact_crater "Impact crater"); during energetic collisions, the entire impactor may be vaporized, leaving no meteorites. [Geologists](/wiki/Geologist "Geologist") use the term "bolide" in a different sense from [astronomers](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") to indicate a very large [impactor](/wiki/Impact_event "Impact event"). For example, the [USGS](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") uses the term to mean a generic large crater-forming projectile in a manner "to imply that we do not know the precise nature of the impacting body ... whether it is a rocky or metallic asteroid, or an icy comet for example".[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Meteoroids also hit other bodies in the Solar System. On such stony bodies as the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") or [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") that have little or no atmosphere, they leave enduring craters.

### Impact craters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Meteoroid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Impact craters")]

Main article: [Impact crater](/wiki/Impact_crater "Impact crater")

Meteoroid collisions with solid Solar System objects, including the Moon, [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"), [Callisto](/wiki/Callisto_(moon) "Callisto (moon)"), [Ganymede](/wiki/Ganymede_(moon) "Ganymede (moon)"), and most small moons and [asteroids](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid"), create impact craters, which are the dominant geographic features of many of those objects. On other planets and moons with active surface geological processes, such as Earth, [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus"), [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars"), [Europa](/wiki/Europa_(moon) "Europa (moon)"), [Io](/wiki/Io_(moon) "Io (moon)"), and [Titan](/wiki/Titan_(moon) "Titan (moon)"), visible impact craters may become [eroded](/wiki/Erosion "Erosion"), buried, or transformed by [tectonics](/wiki/Tectonics "Tectonics") over time. In early literature, before the significance of impact cratering was widely recognised, the terms [cryptoexplosion](/wiki/Cryptoexplosion "Cryptoexplosion") or cryptovolcanic structure were often used to describe what are now recognised as impact-related features on Earth.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Molten terrestrial material ejected from a meteorite impact crater can cool and solidify into an object known as a [tektite](/wiki/Tektite "Tektite"). These are often mistaken for meteorites. Terrestrial rock, sometimes with pieces of the original meteorite, created or modified by an impact of a meteorite is called [impactite](/wiki/Impactite "Impactite").
